sentences.text: Kolodziejek.
Kolodziejek.
OK.
W-A-C-A-- A-- A-W.
Waclaw.
K-O-L-O-D-Z-I-E-J-E-K, Kolodziejek.
Kolodziejek?
Kolodziejek.
In Poland, Wola Korycka.
W-- W-O-L-A K-O-R-I-K-A, Korycka.
In Poland.
January 28, 1922.
Wincenty Kolodziejek.
My father build a castle, a cathedral of Georges.
And also a baron's palaces.
His profession was architect, special architect.
Really, I don't know.
I believe in [INAUDIBLE], under Russian czar-- czar-- [? what ?] czar.
He was very sickly, disciplinary--
I was lucky.
We was happy when he went away from home.
[LAUGHS]
Mostly playing football, and what kind of game, and ride horses.
And beautiful, beautiful life.
And then we went to the forest, pick up mushrooms, blueberries, raspberries during the daytime.
My brother, [INAUDIBLE], Mieczyslaw.
Younger one, and also with a couple girls.
Janina and Teresa, and my brother Mieczyslaw and [?
Eugene. ?]
Mieczyslaw.
He was different-- different person.
I was different from my entire family.
I was very full of energy.
I do so many crazy things.
I went on a tree, apple tree or pear.
I fall down, break the branches.
I was like a monkey, all-- all-- all around.
My mom had so much problem with me.
No, not yet.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Gymnastic.
Especially in the-- when we were in the school, and we do the [? one-hour ?] gymnastic.
I was the best one.
Running, pull-- pulling the rope, jumping; things like that.
Swimming, not so good.
[LAUGHS]
Marching.
Hiking, yes.
No, not me.
I have not-- not the chance.
I believe in Wola Korycka, yes.
My-- I forgot to tell you, my father been remarried.
There was seven of us, seven-- seven from my-- the first mom, which I don't know, because she died.
And there was seven from my-- my mom, 14 children.
In-- [LAUGHS] No, there was a-- my family was all over in Poland.
Not really, mostly I grow up with my mom lately.
When-- and when Marshal Pilsudski died in 1935, I move out from Maciejowice to Warsaw till 1940.
Because it was boring, then it's so much exciting in Warsaw.
Again, so I went to school to Maciejowice.
Yes.
No, my father-- my father had been travel all over in Poland.
So once, every couple of months, he'd come to-- to home.
Yeah.
Yeah.
In Maciejowice.
Then when I move from Maciejowice to Warsaw, I went to special ed school, a night school, to learn German.
Because that was-- in 1940 German occupied Poland.
So that was mostly by-- by force, to go to German-- to school and learn German.
Yes.
Yes.
Kathryn, Kathryn Kolodziejek.
Is [?
Dunek. ?]
I believe also she came from Wola Korycka.
Maybe a different town.
Yes, yes, my mom's sister, she lived in Grabnik.
That's a big city, [NON-ENGLISH] live.
We come to visit her.
Yeah.
Sometime.
Yes.
Yes, she did have children, I believe, two or three.
A little bit younger.
Yeah.
My father was very religious, my mom, but me, I-- I just ignored them, escaped, as a kid, as a younger generation.
Even I can tell you true, when at was Auschwitz, I forgot all about the God.
Because we lived like a-- you know.
Yes, by force, everybody.
I forgot.
The priest in his school, which was giving us lessons one-- one hour a week-- Lucian.
Some way I remember.
He was very good, strictly old-fashioned.
He was very good to-- to help the poor people.
Yeah, but it was not so good, because usually I was-- bad behavior.
So he drove me out in the hall.
Yeah, or sometimes behind the blackboard.
I mean, I'm telling true.
Because, you know, I was young and easy going.
I was good for-- lately for the history, for the religion, but not in the beginning.
Yeah.
Yes.
No, they go in the lately to school.
They was much-- much younger than me.
Yeah.
No, never.
No.
Usually, we go sometimes with the family, sometime with my brother and sister.
Yes.
I believe so, yes.
Yes.
3rd of May, there was a big holiday, a procession.
And a couple of days a year, there was special-- a special holiday, because St.
Mary, special holiday in the evening.
It was very, very, very exciting.
No.
But I went.
Because, you know, everything was so easy, so without control.
Yes, yes.
And we joined too, or watch it, yes.
Beautiful costumes, yes.
Oh, you got me.
I don't know.
I don't pay much attention.
She was excellent seamstress.
Because she-- she sewing uniform for us, and she also is sewing for the neighborhood parents, even for-- my mom was very good to the Jewish family.
Where she-- she'd buy-- buy the fabric from Jewish people, from Maciejowice.
And I remember, I would-- we had three beautiful girls, Jewish, in the school.
And they've been complaining on me.
Because they was sitting on the front of me.
They have beautiful hair.
I pulled it-- I pulled the hair.
I don't remember, really.
The 3rd of May was special holiday.
Special foods, oh, special holiday there was-- like here, on the Christmas time and Easter.
Oh, there was food about for 13, 15, 16 different food on the table.
Dishes.
Special bakery pastry, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] special, my mom bake it.
She was excellent.
And some kind of mushrooms, [INAUDIBLE] and pierogi with sauerkraut, or all kind of different, now delicious, delicious.
Ah, she made an excellent-- my mom even killed the pork, and she made kielbasa, kishka, home-- home-- homemade, excellent.
I never forget it.
And goodies, goodies on the table.
The children-- nothing, nothing, not even my brother, not even me.
We only help to eating.
I believe so, yes.
Yes, she had help.
The truth, we used to live in a-- in a baron property, Petrosky.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had a beautiful life, like a castle.
Maybe 13.
Yes.
White, blue, or something like that.
Big bed.
Toys, small horses, carriage; things like that.
Also some balls.
Nothing, I was not interested in nothing, nothing.
Clearly I start-- when-- when I was liberated from Auschwitz.
Then I woke up.
Yes.
Deblin.
There was Air Force training for the army.
And there was a big [NON-ENGLISH], when Marshal Pilsudski died in 1935 in May, we went to Deblin.
Then after I went to see in Deblin, a beautiful city, so it was a dream for me to immigrate from-- from Maciejowice to Warsaw, which I did.
Jewish people, yes.
I don't think so.
And Polish-Jewish.
In Maciejowice, it was a very well, the Jewish people.
They owned beautiful stores, bakery, fish market, and it was a very, very nice town, Maciejowice.
Yes.
Mostly, mostly all over.
Outside.
School was in Maciejowice, in-- in-- in town.
We walk.
It was not far away, a mile and a half.
And special seven class, there was one school, a big building.
They have to transfer to Deblin, big city, or you even come to Warsaw.
Yeah.
By myself.
[LAUGHS] Even I don't know, but possible I went by train.
With some friends-- my friends.
Yes.
It was like a-- special for the very wealthy people, for the rich family who belonged to the Pilsudski or an organization, like a Legion Pilsudski.
Although I was not from the poor-- from-- from the rich family-- I was like a poor normal.
Yes.
All boys.
I think it was-- it was organized by a-- a wife of the-- [INAUDIBLE] president, she organize.
And there was a couple hundred people.
From all over Poland, mostly from a good family.
Yes.
There was like a-- like a-- a-- West Point cadet.
Like academy.
Yes.
We'd be working, cleaning the rooms, and making clean for the what you call, for the [NON-ENGLISH].
And keep-- keep in good shape, and tip-top everything.
Carpentry, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I was interested.
But, everything was broke under the German occupy.
So we were in different-- different-- different living.
Nothing.
Yeah.
Different interests-- I was-- I was interested very much a little bit of everything.
I-- I went to parents, I visit home.
Maybe once a year.
Through-- though special holiday.
From 19-- about five years, from 1935 to '40.
But-- also Warsaw was occupied by German-- German army.
So everything was broken, like everything.
In school.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's when the German come to Warsaw.
Yes, I work-- I work in the daytime for the [INAUDIBLE], the association with the barons from all over Europe, that's what they called it, [INAUDIBLE].
Incorporated.
I believe that's a German name, [INAUDIBLE].
Messenger, to-- to make them breakfast, and pick up their clothes, like a coat put on hanger, and delivering a message to the manual, or going to post office, et cetera, et cetera.
Only me.
I have this special job, by special recommendation for the barons.
Yes.
I spend mostly for the ice cream and chocolate.
Yes, once in a while.
I don't remember very much.
Not much.
Traveling all around Poland-- not Poland-- Warsaw.
Because before the war, Poland was beautiful.
And so I traveled back and forth.
And I had special pass for the buses and the-- and tramway, Saturdays and Sunday I travel for most often Wilanow.
Wilanow, there's a castle.
I get on at the castle in Wilanow, a beautiful museum.
So I travel during the daytime, Saturday and Sunday.
Because it costs me nothing, only time.
Very much, especially the old town, [INAUDIBLE].
Not far away from the-- from the palace, presidential palace.
But a very historical and beautiful town, even now today.
It was [INAUDIBLE] Street.
And they call it, like [INAUDIBLE], school of [INAUDIBLE], very important, for whatever the draft men.
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE].
I remember, yes.
I'm not sure, 16 or maybe about--
There was many friends.
It was like a-- a [NON-ENGLISH].
That's for the-- for the-- for the poor people, for the poor children.
That's also very disciplinary-- everybody must-- must obey.
Everybody [BACKROUND NOISE] must come in on time.
You had your own chapter.
You would pay very little money, because it mostly was city was-- Warsaw pay.
Yes.
There's many, many people from Lvov, from Krakow, all the people which the-- for the university, for a different-- different training, I was between them.
I was much younger than those people.
Yes, but everything broke over the war.
Oh, excuse me.
I was thinking I was going to join to the Polish army, let's say, there was meetings.
Like West Point.
Too late.
When the German army come to Warsaw, I was scared to death [? and ?] those girls.
No, I was living in the-- in the-- in that school.
And [INAUDIBLE], the wife of the president.
Many people was organized different way, some-- some-- a friend of mine been scared to-- about-- was thinking about joining the underground army.
Yeah.
I had the opportunity, but I was too young, too naive.
I was not even thinking about-- obviously is he going, I say nothing happen to me.
Maybe, maybe a couple of months.
Then I-- completely-- it was like deserted.
I didn't show up anymore in the school.
And I-- I still be working as messenger for the-- for the [INAUDIBLE].
And then I been living-- scary, scary.
I live in a-- a place, [INAUDIBLE].
No, no, with that-- with that group.
But many people been already arrested by German.
And that was kind-- kind of scary place to live, even, at the time.
My family used to live in Maciejowice, [? limited, ?] different town.
They were safe there.
But people who live Warsaw, it was very danger to live day by day.
When the-- when the-- the city was bombed, very much, now this is.
They-- the brought convoy, bread, giving bread to the people.
And they're standing by the-- by the water, watching everybody take the water, for the cooking, for the drinks.
It was organized not so bad.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Yes.
I've been working at-- yes.
I guess I can manage.
We've been very-- I lost track, that many people had been arrested before me.
And many people then I hear actings, who tried to kill German, [INAUDIBLE] too, because it was scary to live in Warsaw.
Yeah.
I heard they've been-- been killed in the street.
There was like a police, a martial law.
They was not-- not allowed to walk in the evening.
And they-- probably some friends of mine ignore it.
No, no.
Yeah, and they got shot.
Many.
